---
title: "Large-Sample Theory"
description: "LLN, CLT, consistency, asymptotic normality, and the Delta method."
---

## Motivation {#sec-asymptotics-motivation}

Finite-sample formulas are elegant, but real data rarely satisfy textbook assumptions exactly. Errors can be heteroskedastic, regressors can be non-Gaussian, and sample sizes can vary from tiny to massive. We still need inference that remains reliable when the model is only approximately correct.

Large-sample theory answers that need. Instead of asking whether an estimator is exact at a fixed $n$, we ask what happens as $n \to \infty$: does the estimator converge to the truth, and at what rate does uncertainty shrink?

This chapter develops the core asymptotic toolkit behind modern applied econometrics: Law of Large Numbers (LLN), Central Limit Theorem (CLT), consistency, asymptotic normality, and the Delta method. These results are the bridge from [OLS Foundations](01-ols.qmd) to robust inference choices in [Standard Errors in Practice](07-standard-errors.qmd).

---

## Convergence Concepts {#sec-asymptotics-concepts}

Let $\{Z_n\}$ be a sequence of random variables and $Z$ a limiting random variable.

- **Convergence in probability**: $Z_n \xrightarrow{p} Z$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$,
  $$
  \mathbb{P}(|Z_n - Z| > \varepsilon) \to 0. \tag{2.1}
  $$
- **Convergence in distribution**: $Z_n \xrightarrow{d} Z$ if CDFs converge at continuity points of $Z$.

Two workhorse rules:

- **Continuous mapping theorem**: if $Z_n \xrightarrow{p} Z$ and $g$ is continuous, then $g(Z_n) \xrightarrow{p} g(Z)$.
- **Slutsky's theorem**: if $A_n \xrightarrow{d} A$ and $B_n \xrightarrow{p} b$ (constant), then $A_n B_n \xrightarrow{d} bA$.

These rules let us move from primitive sample moments to estimators and test statistics.

---

## Law of Large Numbers and Consistency {#sec-asymptotics-lln}

Suppose $W_1,\ldots,W_n$ are i.i.d. with $\mathbb{E}|W_i|<\infty$. Let

$$
\bar W_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n W_i. \tag{2.2}
$$

::: {.callout-note}
## Theorem 2.1 (Weak Law of Large Numbers)

$$
\bar W_n \xrightarrow{p} \mathbb{E}[W_i]. \tag{2.3}
$$
:::

LLN is the engine of consistency. If an estimator can be written as a continuous function of sample averages, and those averages converge to their population counterparts, then the estimator converges to the target parameter.

### Example: OLS Consistency (Sketch) {#sec-asymptotics-ols-consistency}

Notation follows [OLS Foundations](01-ols.qmd): we use $\mathbf{X}$ for the regressor matrix, $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ for the error vector, and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ for the OLS estimator.

In a linear model with exogeneity and full-rank population moments,

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta} = \left(\frac{\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}}{n}\right)^{-1}\frac{\mathbf{X}'\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}{n}. \tag{2.4}
$$

By LLN,

$$
\frac{\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}}{n} \xrightarrow{p} Q_{xx}, \qquad \frac{\mathbf{X}'\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}{n} \xrightarrow{p} 0,
$$

so by continuous mapping,

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \xrightarrow{p} \boldsymbol{\beta}. \tag{2.5}
$$

---

## Central Limit Theorem and Asymptotic Normality {#sec-asymptotics-clt}

LLN gives convergence of point estimates; CLT gives approximate sampling distributions.

::: {.callout-note}
## Theorem 2.2 (Lindeberg-Levy CLT)

If $W_i$ are i.i.d. with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2<\infty$, then

$$
\sqrt{n}(\bar W_n - \mu) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2). \tag{2.6}
$$
:::

For vector moments, the multivariate CLT gives

$$
\sqrt{n}(\bar g_n - g_0) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0,\Omega). \tag{2.7}
$$

This directly yields asymptotic normality of many estimators, including OLS, MLE, and GMM (see [Maximum Likelihood Estimation](09-mle.qmd) and [Generalized Method of Moments](10-gmm.qmd)).

::: {.callout-note}
## Theorem 2.3 (Asymptotic Normality of OLS)

Assume $(Y_i,X_i)$ are i.i.d., $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_i\mid X_i]=0$, $\mathbb{E}\|X_i\varepsilon_i\|^2<\infty$, and $Q_{xx}=\mathbb{E}[X_iX_i']$ is positive definite.

$$
\sqrt{n}(\hat\beta-\beta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0,V), \tag{2.8}
$$

where

$$
V = Q_{xx}^{-1} \Omega Q_{xx}^{-1}, \qquad
Q_{xx}=\mathbb{E}[X_iX_i'], \qquad
\Omega=\mathbb{E}[X_iX_i'\varepsilon_i^2]. \tag{2.9}
$$
:::

Under homoskedasticity, $\Omega=\sigma^2Q_{xx}$ and $V=\sigma^2Q_{xx}^{-1}$. Under heteroskedasticity, use sandwich estimators for $V$; this is exactly why robust/cluster/HAC standard errors are central in practice.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## 📐 Expand proof sketch of Theorem 2.3

**Step 1.** Rewrite the estimation error:

$$
\sqrt{n}(\hat\beta-\beta)=\left(\frac{X'X}{n}\right)^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\varepsilon_i\right). \tag{2.10}
$$

**Step 2.** By LLN, $X'X/n \xrightarrow{p} Q_{xx}$.

**Step 3.** By multivariate CLT,

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\varepsilon_i \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0,\Omega). \tag{2.11}
$$

**Step 4.** Apply Slutsky's theorem to combine both limits and obtain

$$
\sqrt{n}(\hat\beta-\beta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0,Q_{xx}^{-1}\Omega Q_{xx}^{-1}).
$$

$\blacksquare$
:::

---

## Delta Method {#sec-asymptotics-delta}

Often we care about transformed parameters: elasticities, risk ratios, or nonlinear functions of coefficients.

::: {.callout-note}
## Theorem 2.4 (Delta Method)

If

$$
\sqrt{n}(\hat\theta-\theta_0) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma), \tag{2.12}
$$

and $g$ is differentiable at $\theta_0$, then

$$
\sqrt{n}(g(\hat\theta)-g(\theta_0)) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, G\Sigma G'), \tag{2.13}
$$

where $G = \nabla g(\theta_0)'$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## 📐 Expand proof intuition of Theorem 2.4

Use first-order Taylor expansion around $\theta_0$:

$$
g(\hat\theta)=g(\theta_0)+G(\hat\theta-\theta_0)+r_n,
$$

where the remainder $r_n=o_p(n^{-1/2})$. Multiply by $\sqrt{n}$ and apply Slutsky's theorem.

$\blacksquare$
:::

In practice, plug in estimates:

$$
\widehat{\text{Var}}(g(\hat\theta)) = G(\hat\theta)\,\widehat\Sigma\,G(\hat\theta)'. \tag{2.14}
$$

---

## Asymptotic Inference Workflow {#sec-asymptotics-workflow}

For most estimators, inference follows this pattern:

1. Prove consistency via LLN + continuous mapping.
2. Prove asymptotic normality via CLT + Slutsky.
3. Estimate asymptotic variance (homoskedastic, robust, cluster, or HAC).
4. Build Wald-type confidence intervals and tests.

This is why estimator chapters are modular: OLS, MLE, and GMM differ in objective functions, but their large-sample logic is structurally similar.

---

## Interactive Simulation {#sec-asymptotics-simulation}

The simulation below draws repeated samples from a skewed population and shows how the sampling distribution of the mean becomes approximately normal as $n$ grows.

```{shinylive-python}
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 600
#| echo: false

# Simulation: sample size and asymptotic normality of the sample mean

from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

app_ui = ui.page_fillable(
    ui.layout_sidebar(
        ui.sidebar(
            ui.input_slider("n", "Sample size (n)", min=20, max=2000, value=80, step=20),
            ui.input_slider("reps", "Monte Carlo replications", min=200, max=5000, value=1200, step=100),
            ui.input_slider("skew", "Population skew intensity", min=0.5, max=3.0, value=1.5, step=0.1),
            ui.input_action_button("resample", "Resample", class_="btn-primary w-100 mt-2"),
            ui.hr(),
            ui.markdown(
                "**Interpretation:** With larger n, the distribution of "
                "sqrt(n)(mean - mu) gets closer to normal."
            ),
            width=320,
        ),
        ui.output_plot("clt_plot"),
    ),
)


def server(input, output, session):
    @reactive.calc
    def simulate():
        input.resample()
        n = input.n()
        reps = input.reps()
        skew = input.skew()

        # Exponential family -> skewed population
        # Mean = 1/skew, Var = 1/skew^2
        samples = np.random.exponential(scale=1.0 / skew, size=(reps, n))
        means = samples.mean(axis=1)
        mu = 1.0 / skew
        sigma = 1.0 / skew
        z = np.sqrt(n) * (means - mu) / sigma

        return means, z, mu

    @render.plot
    def clt_plot():
        try:
            means, z, mu = simulate()

            fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.4))

            # Left panel: sample mean distribution
            axes[0].hist(means, bins=35, density=True, color="#4a7fb5", alpha=0.75,
                         edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.5)
            axes[0].axvline(mu, color="#2d2d2d", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label="True mean")
            axes[0].set_title("Sampling distribution of mean", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
            axes[0].set_xlabel("Sample mean", fontsize=11)
            axes[0].set_ylabel("Density", fontsize=11)
            axes[0].legend(fontsize=8.5)

            # Right panel: standardized means vs normal
            axes[1].hist(z, bins=35, density=True, color="#7c9f65", alpha=0.75,
                         edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.5, label="Simulated")
            x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 400)
            normal_pdf = (1.0 / np.sqrt(2.0 * np.pi)) * np.exp(-0.5 * x**2)
            axes[1].plot(x, normal_pdf, color="#d94f4f", linewidth=2.0, label="N(0,1)")
            axes[1].set_title("Standardized mean and CLT", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
            axes[1].set_xlabel("sqrt(n) * (mean - mu) / sigma", fontsize=11)
            axes[1].set_ylabel("Density", fontsize=11)
            axes[1].legend(fontsize=8.5)

            # Summary box
            txt = (
                "E[mean] (sim) = {:.4f}\n"
                "SD[mean] (sim)= {:.4f}\n"
                "mean(z)      = {:.4f}\n"
                "sd(z)        = {:.4f}"
            ).format(
                means.mean(),
                means.std(ddof=1),
                z.mean(),
                z.std(ddof=1),
            )
            axes[1].text(
                0.03, 0.97, txt,
                transform=axes[1].transAxes,
                fontsize=8.5,
                va="top",
                fontfamily="monospace",
                bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.4", facecolor="white", edgecolor="#cccccc", alpha=0.95),
            )

            plt.tight_layout()
            return fig
        except Exception as err:
            fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3))
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.text(0.02, 0.75, "Simulation error", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
            ax.text(0.02, 0.50, str(err), fontsize=10)
            ax.text(0.02, 0.25, "Click Resample after adjusting parameters.", fontsize=10)
            return fig


app = App(app_ui, server)
```

---

## Summary {#sec-asymptotics-summary}

| Result | Required Assumptions | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| Theorem 2.1 (LLN) | i.i.d. + finite first moment | Sample averages converge in probability to population moments |
| OLS consistency sketch | Exogeneity + full rank + LLN conditions | $\hat\beta \xrightarrow{p} \beta$ |
| Theorem 2.2 (CLT) | i.i.d. + finite variance | Centered and scaled sample averages converge to normal |
| Theorem 2.3 (OLS asymptotic normality) | LLN + CLT + regularity conditions | $\sqrt{n}(\hat\beta-\beta)$ is asymptotically Gaussian |
| Theorem 2.4 (Delta method) | Differentiable transform + asymptotic normal base estimator | Nonlinear functions of estimators inherit asymptotic normality |

Large-sample theory is the common language across modern econometrics: first show convergence, then characterize uncertainty, then choose a variance estimator appropriate to the data-generating environment.
